explanation_en: Use 是 to connect a person with an identity, role, or name. This is the first output pattern for introductions.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ hǎo, wǒ shì Lǐ Míng.
drill_examples.translation_en: Hello, I am Li Ming.
My name is Wang Fang.
Are you a student?
He is a teacher.
She is my friend.
We are friends.
What is your name?
I am Korean.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Add 嗎 to the end of a statement to make a yes/no question. Keep the word order the same.

drill_examples.translation_en: Are you a teacher?
Is he a student?
This is one.
Is this three?
Five plus five is ten.
One, two, three, four, five.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yǒu wǔ ge péngyǒu ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Do you have five friends?
I am number two.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 的 after a pronoun or noun to show possession: 我的手機, 他的書, 我們的家.

drill_examples.translation_en: I have two books.
This is my phone.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nà shì nǐ de shū ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Is that your book?
He has eight friends.
Her phone is good.
We have nine books.
Is this one hundred?
My friend has a phone.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Mandarin often puts time before the subject or right after it. Use 今天, 明天, and 昨天 to anchor the sentence.

drill_examples.translation_en: Today I am at home.
Tomorrow I go to school.
Mom was at home yesterday.
My dad is a teacher.
My older sister has a phone.
His younger brother is a student.
My younger sister is happy today.
My older brother comes to my home tomorrow.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 個 as a general measure word for people. For family size, say 我家有 + number + 個人.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ jiā yǒu wǔ ge rén.
drill_examples.translation_en: There are five people in my family.

drill_examples.text: 他有一個姐姐和兩個弟弟。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā yǒu yí ge jiějie hé liǎng ge dìdi.
drill_examples.translation_en: He has one older sister and two younger brothers.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 누나 한 명과 남동생 두 명이 있습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: I have twelve friends.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ jiā yǒu jǐ ge rén?
drill_examples.translation_en: How many people are in your family?
I am twenty years old this year.
There are fifteen students here.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen jiā yǒu yí ge lǎoshī.
drill_examples.translation_en: Our family has one teacher.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리 가족에는 선생님 한 명이 있습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: He is my friend.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 想 before a verb to say what you want to do: 我想喝茶, 我想吃麵.

drill_examples.translation_en: I want to drink tea.

drill_examples.pinyin: Tā è le, tā xiǎng chī miàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: He is hungry; he wants to eat noodles.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 배고파서 면을 먹고 싶어 합니다.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ kě le, wǒ xiǎng hē shuǐ.
drill_examples.translation_en: I am thirsty; I want to drink water.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 목이 말라서 물을 마시고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Do you want to drink coffee?
I want to eat rice.
She does not want to drink coffee.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 커피를 마시고 싶어 하지 않습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Today we want to eat noodles.
Dad wants to drink tea.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Combine the week one patterns into a short spoken exchange. The goal is fluency, not new grammar.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ hǎo, nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?
drill_examples.translation_en: Hello, what is your name?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ jiào Wáng Fāng. Nǐ ne?
drill_examples.translation_en: My name is Wang Fang. And you?
I am Li Qiang.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ jiā yǒu jǐ ge rén?
drill_examples.translation_en: How many people are in your family?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ jiā yǒu sì ge rén.
drill_examples.translation_en: There are four people in my family.
What do you want to do today?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng hē chá, yě xiǎng chī miàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: I want to drink tea and also eat noodles.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 차를 마시고 면도 먹고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Are we going to school tomorrow?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 要 for a practical ordering sentence. In Taiwan, 我要... is common and direct in shops and restaurants.

drill_examples.translation_en: I would like noodles and tea.
Do you want milk?
I want to eat eggs.
This dish is tasty.

drill_examples.pinyin: Bàba xiǎng chī yú hé mǐfàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: Dad wants to eat fish and rice.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 아빠는 생선과 밥을 먹고 싶어 합니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: I do not eat meat.
We go to a restaurant to eat.
My younger sister likes apples and fruit.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Mandarin verbs do not conjugate. Build output by swapping subjects, verbs, and useful objects.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng qù xuéxiào xué Zhōngwén.
drill_examples.translation_en: I want to go to school to study Chinese.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 학교에 가서 중국어를 배우고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: He can write Chinese characters.
We listen to music.
She likes watching movies.
Can you speak Chinese?
Today I want to write my name.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 오늘 저는 제 이름을 쓰고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Older brother cooks.
I do not want to watch a movie.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

pattern: Question word stays in answer position
explanation_en: Put the question word where the answer would go. Do not move it to the front like English.

drill_examples.translation_en: What work do you do?
Who is he?
Where are you going?
Why do you study Chinese?
How do you say this character?
What is her name?
Where is the restaurant?
What place do you like?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 不 for general negation and 沒有 for not having something. For 有, the negative is 沒有.

values.meaning_en: do not have; there is not

drill_examples.translation_en: I do not drink coffee.
He does not have a cat.
I do not want to eat meat.
She does not have a phone.
We are not going to school today.
Dad also does not drink tea.
They all do not have books.
Do you not like movies?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 很 before adjectives in basic descriptive sentences. It often acts like a natural link, not always a strong 'very'.

drill_examples.translation_en: It is hot today.
His older sister is pretty.
This restaurant is expensive.
My home is small.
Your Chinese is good.
Today is not cold.
He has many friends.
My younger sister is happy.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Mandarin counts nouns with measure words. Start with 個 for people, 本 for books, 隻 for animals, and 杯 for cups.

drill_examples.translation_en: I have three books.
He has two cats.
I would like a cup of water.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yào liǎng bēi chá ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Do you want two cups of tea?
There are five books here.
My younger sister does not have a dog.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng mǎi yì běn shū.
drill_examples.translation_en: I want to buy one book.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 책 한 권을 사고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: We would like three cups of coffee.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use week two words to order food, ask preferences, and count items with measure words.

drill_examples.translation_en: What do you want to eat?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng chī yú, yě xiǎng chī cài.
drill_examples.translation_en: I want to eat fish and also vegetables.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 생선도 먹고 채소도 먹고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Do you drink tea or coffee?
I would like one cup of tea.
Is this dish expensive?
I do not eat meat.
This restaurant is good.

drill_examples.text: 我們要兩杯水和一份麵。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yào liǎng bēi shuǐ hé yí fèn miàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: We would like two cups of water and one serving of noodles.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 물 두 잔과 면 하나 주세요.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 現在幾點 and 今天星期幾 for practical time questions. Time words still come early in the sentence.

drill_examples.translation_en: What time is it now?
It is three o'clock now.
What day of the week is today?
Tomorrow is Wednesday.
I study Chinese in the morning.
In the afternoon we go to a restaurant.
I am twenty this year.
In the morning I drink coffee.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 呢 to bounce a question back or ask 'what about...?' Use 幾 for small countable numbers and 多少 for open quantities or prices.

drill_examples.translation_en: I am good. How about you?
How old are you?
How much does this cost?
One cup of coffee is fifty dollars.
What date is today?

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ jiā yǒu jǐ ge rén?
drill_examples.translation_en: How many people are in your family?
How much is this book?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng mǎi píngguǒ, nǐ ne?
drill_examples.translation_en: I want to buy apples. How about you?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

pattern: 我可以 + verb phrase + 嗎？
explanation_en: Use 可以 before a verb to ask if something is allowed. It is a practical pattern for Taiwan shops, classrooms, and travel.

drill_examples.translation_en: May I sit here?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ kěyǐ yòng nǐ de shǒujī ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: May I use your phone?
May I ask something?

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ kěyǐ shuō màn yìdiǎn ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Can you speak a little slower?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 조금 천천히 말해 줄 수 있습니까?

drill_examples.translation_en: May I try it?
Can we drink coffee?
Can people sit here?
Do you have a question?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 喜歡 for likes and 愛 for stronger preferences. Add 最 to say what someone likes the most.

drill_examples.translation_en: I like watching movies.

drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zuì ài chī Táiwān xiǎochī.
drill_examples.translation_en: He loves Taiwanese snacks the most.
She likes listening to music.
I do not like drinking coffee.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 커피 마시는 것을 좋아하지 않습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Do you like taking photos?
We love Taiwan.
Dad likes walking.
I like tea the most.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 先, 然後, 再, and 最後 to organize a spoken sequence. This is the bridge from single sentences to short stories.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng xiān hē shuǐ, zài xué Zhōngwén.
drill_examples.translation_en: I want to drink water first, then study Chinese.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 먼저 물을 마시고 그다음 중국어를 공부하고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.text: 他早上起床，然後吃早餐。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zǎoshang qǐchuáng, ránhòu chī zǎocān.
drill_examples.translation_en: He gets up in the morning, then eats breakfast.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 아침에 일어나고 그다음 아침을 먹습니다.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiān qù gōngsī, zài qù cāntīng.
drill_examples.translation_en: I go to the office first, then to a restaurant.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 먼저 회사에 가고 그다음 식당에 갑니다.

drill_examples.text: 我們先看電影，然後喝咖啡。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xiān kàn diànyǐng, ránhòu hē kāfēi.
drill_examples.translation_en: We watch a movie first, then drink coffee.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리는 먼저 영화를 보고 그다음 커피를 마십니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Lastly, I go home.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ xiān shuō, wǒ zài xiě.
drill_examples.translation_en: You speak first; then I write.

drill_examples.pinyin: Tā xiān mǎi shū, zài qù xuéxiào.
drill_examples.translation_en: She buys a book first, then goes to school.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 먼저 책을 사고 그다음 학교에 갑니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Lastly we go to the park to walk.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 在 before a place to say where an action happens. For positions, use A 在 B 的 left/right/front.

drill_examples.translation_en: I am reading at the library.
The school is to the left of the hospital.
The supermarket is in front of our home.
The phone is on the table.
The book is under the chair.
Where do you work?
I wait for you here.
He drinks coffee there.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Plan a small outing by combining time, location, permission, preference, and sequence patterns.

drill_examples.text: 明天下午三點我們在公園見面。
drill_examples.pinyin: Míngtiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn wǒmen zài gōngyuán jiànmiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: Tomorrow at 3 PM we meet at the park.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 내일 오후 세 시에 우리는 공원에서 만납니다.

drill_examples.text: 然後我們去咖啡店喝咖啡。
drill_examples.pinyin: Ránhòu wǒmen qù kāfēi diàn hē kāfēi.
drill_examples.translation_en: Then we go to a cafe to drink coffee.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그다음 우리는 카페에 가서 커피를 마십니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Can you sit here?
I want to watch a movie first.
She does not like drinking coffee.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 커피 마시는 것을 좋아하지 않습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Lastly we go to the library.
Where is the library?
Today's plan is good.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 去 for going away from the speaker and 來 for coming toward the speaker. Add transport with 坐.

drill_examples.translation_en: We will go to the park for a walk this afternoon.

drill_examples.pinyin: Tā míngtiān zuò huǒchē lái Táizhōng.
drill_examples.translation_en: He comes to Taichung by train tomorrow.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 내일 기차를 타고 타이중에 옵니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Are you going to the shop?
Where is the hospital?
I take the MRT to school.
The station is in front of the park.
Dad takes a plane to Taipei.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ míngtiān lái wǒ jiā ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Are you coming to my home tomorrow?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 比 to compare two things. The adjective comes after the compared item: 今天比昨天熱.

drill_examples.translation_en: Today is hotter than yesterday.

drill_examples.pinyin: Zhège cài bǐ nàge cài guì.
drill_examples.translation_en: This dish is more expensive than that dish.
Is the MRT faster than the train?

drill_examples.text: 這杯咖啡比那杯茶便宜。
drill_examples.pinyin: Zhè bēi kāfēi bǐ nà bēi chá piányi.
drill_examples.translation_en: This coffee is cheaper than that tea.

drill_examples.text: 我的手機比你的手機新。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ de shǒujī bǐ nǐ de shǒujī xīn.
drill_examples.translation_en: My phone is newer than your phone.
Today is colder than yesterday.

drill_examples.pinyin: Tā shuō Zhōngwén bǐ wǒ hǎo.
drill_examples.translation_en: He speaks Chinese better than me.
This restaurant is even more expensive.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

pattern: A + 在 + B + 的 + direction
explanation_en: Use 在...的... to say where one place is relative to another place.

drill_examples.translation_en: The bank is in front of the shop.
The library is behind the school.
Where is the bathroom?
The phone is inside the bag.
He is waiting for you outside.

drill_examples.pinyin: Yīyuàn zài zhè tiáo lù de yòubiān.
drill_examples.translation_en: The hospital is on the right side of this road.
The park is to the left of the station.
The restaurant is behind the bank.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

pattern: 我需要 / 我得 / 我必須 + action
explanation_en: Use 需要 for 'need to', 得 for everyday 'have to', and 必須 for stronger 'must'.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xūyào qù yínháng qǔ qián.
drill_examples.translation_en: I need to go to the bank to withdraw money.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 은행에 가서 돈을 찾아야 합니다.

drill_examples.pinyin: Bàoqiàn, xiànzài wǒ bìxū zǒu le.
drill_examples.translation_en: Sorry, I must leave now.
I have to study Chinese.
Do you need a receipt?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xūyào yì zhāng Yōuyóu Kǎ.
drill_examples.translation_en: I need an EasyCard.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen děi zuò jiéyùn qù xuéxiào.
drill_examples.translation_en: We have to take the MRT to school.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리는 MRT를 타고 학교에 가야 합니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: He must go to the office in the morning.
I need to take my phone.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 請 before a command or invitation to soften it. Combine with 可以嗎 for a polite check.

drill_examples.translation_en: Please come in!

drill_examples.pinyin: Qǐng bāng wǒ yíxià, kěyǐ ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Please help me for a moment, is that okay?
Please come with me.
Please wait a moment.
Please give me the menu.
I would like a bag.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ xiǎng yìqǐ hē kāfēi ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Do you want to drink coffee together?

drill_examples.pinyin: Kěyǐ qǐng nǐ shuō màn yìdiǎn ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Could you please speak a little slower?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 조금 천천히 말해 주실 수 있습니까?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use 愛好 for hobbies and combine 喜歡/愛 with activities to talk about free time.

drill_examples.text: 我的愛好是唱歌和跳舞。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ de àihào shì chànggē hé tiàowǔ.
drill_examples.translation_en: My hobbies are singing and dancing.

drill_examples.text: 我喜歡旅遊，也喜歡拍照。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xǐhuan lǚyóu, yě xǐhuan pāizhào.
drill_examples.translation_en: I like traveling and also taking photos.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 여행도 좋아하고 사진 찍기도 좋아합니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: He does not like exercising.
Can you swim?
My younger sister loves singing the most.
We go to the park to run in the afternoon.
My friend likes dancing.
What is your hobby?

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

pattern: Subject + 會 / 要 / 打算 + action
explanation_en: Use 會 for will/can, 要 for going to, 想 for intent, and 打算 for plans.

drill_examples.translation_en: Tomorrow I will go to Taipei.
I want to learn to swim.

drill_examples.text: 我們打算下個星期見面。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen dǎsuàn xià ge xīngqī jiànmiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: We plan to meet next week.
He hopes to learn Chinese well.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 중국어를 잘 배우고 싶어 합니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: What are you going to do tomorrow?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ huì zuò jiéyùn qù gōngsī.
drill_examples.translation_en: I will take the MRT to the office.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 MRT를 타고 회사에 갈 것입니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: Our plan is good.
She does not plan to drink coffee.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: Use this day to turn separate sentence patterns into a short daily routine paragraph.

values.meaning_en: do not have; there is not

drill_examples.pinyin: Jīntiān zǎoshang wǒ xiān hē shuǐ.
drill_examples.translation_en: This morning I drink water first.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 오늘 아침 저는 먼저 물을 마십니다.

drill_examples.pinyin: Ránhòu wǒ zài jiā xué Zhōngwén.
drill_examples.translation_en: Then I study Chinese at home.
In the afternoon I go to the office to work.

drill_examples.text: 我不喝咖啡，因為我想喝茶。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ bù hē kāfēi, yīnwèi wǒ xiǎng hē chá.
drill_examples.translation_en: I do not drink coffee because I want tea.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 차를 마시고 싶어서 커피를 마시지 않습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: In the evening I do not have class.
I read at the library.
Finally I go home to eat.

drill_examples.text: 我的一天很忙，但是很好。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ de yì tiān hěn máng, dànshì hěn hǎo.
drill_examples.translation_en: My day is busy, but good.

negative_examples.error: Use the Mandarin pattern shown above instead of translating word-for-word.

explanation_en: This final Month 1 loop combines the most usable output patterns into a five-minute conversation.

drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ hǎo, wǒ jiào Lǐ Míng.
drill_examples.translation_en: Hello, my name is Li Ming.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ jiā yǒu sì ge rén.
drill_examples.translation_en: There are four people in my family.
I like Taiwanese snacks.

drill_examples.pinyin: Jīntiān xiàwǔ wǒ xiǎng qù gōngyuán.
drill_examples.translation_en: This afternoon I want to go to the park.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 오늘 오후 저는 공원에 가고 싶습니다.

drill_examples.translation_en: May I sit here?
The library is behind the school.
Today is hotter than yesterday.

drill_examples.text: 明天我打算坐捷運去台北。
drill_examples.pinyin: Míngtiān wǒ dǎsuàn zuò jiéyùn qù Táiběi.
drill_examples.translation_en: Tomorrow I plan to take the MRT to Taipei.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 내일 저는 MRT를 타고 타이베이에 갈 계획입니다.
